issue_title: dcc file transfer (receiving) decode error
issue_body:
i used the script "dccreceive.py"
and when receiving a file larger then 1151 bytes (or the received byte excede this)

i tested this simply by trial and error on struct.pack("!I", bytes).encode("utf-8")
it fails on:

```
#!python

>>> struct.pack("!I", 1152).encode("utf-8")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf8' codec can't decode byte 0x80 in position 3: invalid start byte
```

the private message that is returned with the received bytes can not be encoded with utf-8

the message that is supposed to be send is a string from struct.pack()
e.g. 

```
#!python
struct.pack("!I", bytesReceived)

```
that output is given to the dcc privatemsg function
where it is being encoded to utf-8

```
#!python
string = struct.pack("!I", bytesReceived)
bytes = string.encode('utf-8')
```
now with small bytesReceived this works:
```
#!python
>>> struct.pack("!I", 14).encode("utf-8")
'\x00\x00\x00\x0e'
```
but
```
#!python
>>> struct.pack("!I", 1440).encode("utf-8")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf8' codec can't decode byte 0xa0 in position 3: invalid start byte
```
i checked what "0xa0" is and its a none breaking space and apparently a utf-8 string is not allowed to start with that!

i dont understand why anything from struct.pack() is not utf-8 encodable since it should be a string representing bytes not a byte string

```
#!python
>>> type(struct.pack("!I", 14).encode("utf-8"))
<type 'str'>
```

so in conclusion i dont know if this is an error in struct.pack() or anywhere else, besides is it needed to encode the message at all ?

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/jaraco/irc/issue/24
- Originally reported by: Dennis Lutter
- Originally created at: 2013-07-11T19:13:57.215

issue_title: Broken pypi metadata
issue_body:
The `mixer` metadata is broken in https://pypi.python.org/pypi/mixer.

One bad side effect of this is the check of python3 compatibility that [canIusepython3](https://caniusepython3.com/project/mixer) does. It uses the classifiers to search for python 3. It's a false negative caused by the broken metadata.

Best regards!

What kind of complexity does React introduce in your opinion? Have you considered UMD builds (these are just good old "classic" way of packaging a module into a single .js file with globally exposed interfaces) of ReactJS? You may also like Preact and Inferno projects (they are extremely lightweight ReactJS implementations).

issue_title: Fetch disassembly broken: -data-disassemble: Invalid filename.  (probably gdb bug)
issue_body:
I'm on x86-64 Arch Linux (rolling release, updated last week), using python-gdbgui 0.7.9.5-1 built from https://aur.archlinux.org/packages/python-gdbgui/.

I have gdb 8.0 (from the Arch binary package), and my browser is Chromium 60.0.3112.101 (Developer Build) (64-bit) from the Arch binary package.

----

Disassembly output works with a hello world compiled with gcc, clang, g++, or clang++.

But on a program compiled and linked with `-pthread` (which does actually *use* a pthread function), I get an error.

Clicking "fetch disassembly" (even before the running the program) produces an error message in the gdb console:
`-data-disassemble: Invalid filename.`  The message is also displayed in upper bar next to the a red "error" tag.

This happens with g++, clang++, and gcc and clang.  (My program is valid C++11 and valid C11, because I was testing alignment of `atomic_llong` is structs in 32-bit mode.  gcc under-aligns them so they're not actually atomic.)

Here's an example build command: `g++ -g -std=gnu++11 -pthread -march=native -O3 atomic-align64-test.c -o atomic-align64-test.gcc.64`.  (native in this case is `-march=skylake`).  The same problem happens with `-m64` or `-m32`.

I used `strace -s1024 -p $(pidof gdb)` to see what command gdb actually received.

issue_title: raising TryAgain when the retrying should be stopped causes deadlock
issue_body:
I have a simple function - it checks if a request result is a certain value and if not raises `TryAgain` to try again.
I'm not sure why, but after the function should stop retrying, it enters a deadlock when `TryAgain` is raised - no new attempts are made and function doesn't return.
This simple example demonstrate it:
```python
@tenacity.retry(stop=stop_after_delay(10),wait=wait_fixed(1))
def some_function():
    raise tenacity.TryAgain()
```
I'm using python 3.5.2.

issue_title: Продвинутый вариант Class Based View (CBV)
Feature Request: run() method in FlowerCommand
issue_body:
Celery's `worker` `Command` has a nice `run(...)` method ([here](https://github.com/celery/celery/blob/master/celery/bin/worker.py#L230)) that allows you to start a worker from Python, rather than the command.  This is nice for a number of reasons, we mainly use it for starting workers inside integration tests.  We are adding Flower to our program, and it would be nice to have a similar `run(...)` method.  This is in comparison to what I have put together for now (unless I'm missing something obvious in the code):
```
app = Celery(...)
flower = FlowerCommand(app)
flower.run_from_argv("flower", argv="--port={}".format(celery_port))
```

Would there be some interest in this?

issue_title: Make augment_images return augmentation parameters instead of augmenting the images
issue_body:
I just landed at this package and am amazed by the functionality it provides. Will be using it a lot on my computer vision + machine learning projects.

I was just trying to use it to augment the images on one of my projects and I have a suggestion for an additional feature.

The `imagaug.augmenters.Sequential` currently has an `augment_images`  that takes a bunch of images and augments each of them using randomly drawn augmentation parameters for each of its child augmenters.

Instead of returning the augmented images which takes more memory is it possible to get only the randomly chosen augmentations parameters for each image?

I would like to just store these parameters along with the path to the image, and whenever i load the image, i would like to call an augment_image function with the corresponding augmentation parameters to get the augmented_image.

issue_title: Tutorial introduction example (with a change)
issue_body:
[Curio Tutorial](https://curio.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial.html) uses the following example to introduce some of its concepts. 

```py
# hello.py                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
import curio                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
async def friend(name):                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    print('Hi, my name is', name)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    print('Playing Minecraft')                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
    try:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
        await curio.sleep(1000)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
    except curio.CancelledError:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
        print(name, 'going home')                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
        raise                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
async def countdown(n):                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    while n > 0:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
        print('T-minus', n)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
        await curio.sleep(1)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
        n -= 1                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
async def kid():                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
    print('Building the Millenium Falcon in Minecraft')                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
    async with curio.TaskGroup() as f:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
        await f.spawn(friend, 'Max')                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        
        await f.spawn(friend, 'Lillian')                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
        await f.spawn(friend, 'Thomas')                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
    try:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
        await curio.sleep(1000)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
    except curio.CancelledError:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
        print('Fine. Saving my work.')                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
        raise                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
async def parent():                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
    kid_task = await curio.spawn(kid)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
    await curio.sleep(5)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
    print("Let's go")                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
    count_task = await curio.spawn(countdown, 10)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
    await count_task.join()                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
    print("We're leaving!")                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
    try:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
        await curio.timeout_after(10, kid_task.join)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        
    except curio.TaskTimeout:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               
        print('I warned you!')                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
        await kid_task.cancel()                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
    print('Leaving!')                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
if __name__ == '__main__':                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
    curio.run(parent)
```

The above code is not exactly similar to the example mentioned on the page, `kid()` function has one change. `try/except` block is outside the `async with` block. 

The above example produces the following output:

```
Building the Millenium Falcon in Minecraft
Hi, my name is Max
Playing Minecraft
Hi, my name is Lillian
Playing Minecraft
Hi, my name is Thomas
Playing Minecraft
Let's go
T-minus 10
T-minus 9
T-minus 8
T-minus 7
T-minus 6
T-minus 5
T-minus 4
T-minus 3
T-minus 2
T-minus 1
We're leaving!
I warned you!
Max going home
Thomas going home
Lillian going home
Leaving!
```

As you can see, the line "Fine. Saving my work." never gets printed. (All the friends got the `CancelledError` exception and printed `... going home` fine.

Is this intential behaviour? If yes, this is slighly surprising.

issue_title: Create SUPPORTED_PROFILES.md

issue_title: `from __future__ import unicode_literals` breaks fysom
issue_body:
The `unicode_literals` feature is a way to get Python 2.7 code to behave a little more like 3.x code by assuming that string literals are Unicode strings rather than byte strings.

Unfortunately, it breaks `fysom`:

```
RC=0 stuartl@rikishi /tmp/fysom $ cat test.py 
from __future__ import unicode_literals
from fysom import Fysom

fysom = Fysom({
            'initial': 'green',
            'events': [
                {'name': 'warn', 'src': 'green', 'dst': 'yellow'},
                {'name': 'panic', 'src': 'yellow', 'dst': 'red'},
                {'name': 'calm', 'src': 'red', 'dst': 'yellow'},
                {'name': 'clear', 'src': 'yellow', 'dst': 'green'},
                {'name': 'warm', 'src': 'green', 'dst': 'blue'}
            ]
        })
RC=0 stuartl@rikishi /tmp/fysom $ python3 test.py 
RC=0 stuartl@rikishi /tmp/fysom $ python2 test.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 11, in <module>
    {'name': 'warm', 'src': 'green', 'dst': 'blue'}
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/fysom/__init__.py", line 124, in __init__
    self._apply(cfg)
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/fysom/__init__.py", line 195, in _apply
    setattr(self, name, self._build_event(name))
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/fysom/__init__.py", line 261, in _build_event
    fn.__name__ = event
TypeError: __name__ must be set to a string object
```

issue_title: datetime type detection is not good enough
issue_body:
Right now, the type detection does infer a date, datetime or time types without taking into account the fact that `01/02/2002` can be both *Februrary the 1st* or *January the 2nd* depending on the date format used respectively `DD/MM/YYYY` and `MM/DD/YYYY`.

This might be undecidable in some rare cases, but in general it's possible given enough values to decide between both formats.

One possible way, to handle this in `meza` is to use a higher level datatype for representing the type of a field to replace the current string representation. For instance:

```python
datetime_type = namedtuple('DateTimeType', ['format'])
```

Basically, use a representation that takes optional extra information about the type.

issue_title: Makefile export
issue_body: It seems that the function save_makefile which I am mostly interested in, has not been invoked in the source code and export to Makefile doesn't work

issue_title: built-in vars() overwritten
issue_body:
I like using vars as a short hand notation in Fit and other objects. I must admit that I didn't realise it was a built-in at first. This might be a potential issue, so I should consider renaming this in future functions.

Luckily this does not influence the user API.

issue_title: self.sigmas identical to self.sigma_data
issue_body: These two seem to contain the same information (on `Fit` objects). To prevent future bugs from popping up, this needs to be checked more carefully and if so, only self.sigma_data should be kept.

issue_title: camera orientation of viewer
issue_body:
I was wondering why you are viewing down the z axis (or viewing the x-y plane) in nglviewer.
I find it more intuitive the way VMD does it viewing the x-z plane. Especially since most membrane systems are typically built with the membrane on the x-y plane and z positive looking up.
So every time I build a membrane system I need to rotate all the way around to see my molecule.

Is there maybe an option for changing the default camera orientation?

issue_title: daily_forecast("Milan,it") fails with free API key
issue_body:
Thanks for the library, it's been super helpful. 

The Readme page example code failed with an invalid API key error. Not sure if they changed the pricing plan recently, but the daily forecast is not part of the free API, it would be great to have this commented because the rest of the library is so easy to get started with.

issue_title: Make zulu.Delta's arithmetic magic methods return Delta objects instead of datetime.timedelta
issue_body:
Due to the way `datetime.timedelta` is implemented, the `zulu.Delta` subclass isn't used:

```python
>>> delta = zulu.Delta(seconds=5)
>>> delta + delta
datetime.timedelta(0, 10)
>>> delta - delta
datetime.timedelta(0)
>>> delta / 1
datetime.timedelta(0, 5)
>>> delta // 1
datetime.timedelta(0, 5)
>>> delta * 1
datetime.timedelta(0, 5)
```

issue_title: Support FreeBSD (currently - RuntimeError no suitable implementation)
issue_body:
Relevant code in similar library: https://github.com/gavinbeatty/python-monotonic-time/blob/master/monotonic_time.py.in#L59

Boils down to:
* Linux `clock_gettime(1, &ts)`
* FreeBSD `clock_gettime(4, &ts)`

where 4 is also `#define CLOCK_MONOTONIC	4` in https://github.com/freebsd/freebsd/blob/master/include/time.h

Please, add support for FreeBSD.

issue_title: doesn't check ssl certificate hostname
issue_body:
Hello,

If I try to connect using START_TLS to a server using its IP address instead of its hostname, it still works.

This is despite the fact that only the hostname is included in the certificate.

This allows man in the middle attacks because I might be trying to connect to server.example.org, but get a valid certificate from maninthemiddle.example.org instead, and I will send my credentials to the wrong server.

Ideally this needs to be configurable, as I do have some broken systems that do identify themselves with the wrong name.

This is with validate = ssl.CERT_REQUIRED

Looking at the code, it looks like it is suppose to check the certificate in the check_hostname function, so I am going to try and work out why that doesn't appear to be working (or if I am doing something wrong), and I will report back here.

Thanks

issue_title: ordering of singular values by svd()
issue_body:
The singular values returned by svd() (when not compiled with LAPACK support) are in no particular order - as per the docs. However, when compiled with LAPACK support, or when running other similar functions (e.g. svd_fast()), the singular values **are** ordered. This obviously affects the other returned matrices as well.

There is no error with the output - the matrices returned by svd() are consistent. But I think it is unexpected that the result returned by the function is dramatically different depending on whether or not dlib was compiled with LAPACK support.

issue_title: `fix_normals` On multiple meshes where one mesh is watertight does not fix normals on watertight meshes in volume.
issue_body:
Basically, if there are two discrete cuboids in a trimesh object `m`, but one of them is missing a face, and you run `m.fix_normals()`, then because the *whole mesh* isn't watertight, none of the normals are repaired, even on the submesh that *is* watertight.

i've come up with the following workaround:
```python
def get_normals_fixed_mesh_submesh_split(m, only_watertight=False):
    """
    Splits a mesh into submeshes, and fixes normals/winding of all submeshes.

    PARAMS:
        m: Trimesh, The mesh to fix the normals of.
        [only_watertight]: bool, Whether to only fix normals of watertight submeshes.

    RETURNS:
        normals_fixed_m: Trimesh, The fixed version of m.
    """
    subs = m.split(only_watertight=only_watertight)
    [sub.fix_normals() for sub in subs]
    first = subs[0]
    for sub in subs[1:]:
        first += sub
    return first
```

issue_title: Snapcraft.yaml for httpstat
issue_body:
I am a user of httpstat and I used httpstat as an example to show how to package software as snaps (https://www.ubuntu.com/desktop/snappy). 

To make a snap for a software package, you need to create a snapcraft.yaml configuration file. 
Here is the one for httpstat: https://gist.github.com/simos/3214bbd2f7d4a4647918bdb2afb926cd

How to create the snap?
1. Install snapcraft 2.26 (default in Ubuntu 16.04 or newer)
2. Run "snapcraft" in the same directory that has the snapcraft.yaml from the gist.
3. The httpstat_1.1.3_amd64.snap will be produced. 

I wrote an article about all this at https://blog.simos.info/how-to-create-a-snap-for-a-python-app-with-networking-using-snapcraft-in-ubuntu-16-04/

I understand that someone would simply need to grab the .py file and run it. I am posting this issue to inform that I have used "httpstat" as an example for my tutorial.

issue_title: UKF and scaled UKF are not consistent with each other
issue_body:

issue_body:
Hello,

I am trying to use plyvel 0.9 on a Ubuntu 16.04.4 LTS machine with Python 3.5.2-2ubuntu0~16.04.1.
I expected it to work, but I get an ImportError.

I did the following:

1. I installed leveldb with `apt install libleveldb-dev` which installed the following packages:
`libleveldb-dev:amd64                1.18-5 `
`libleveldb1v5:amd64                 1.18-5`

2. I installed plyvel with `pip3 install plyvel`:
```
$ sudo pip3 install plyvel
Collecting plyvel
Installing collected packages: plyvel
Successfully installed plyvel-0.9
You are using pip version 8.1.1, however version 9.0.1 is available.
You should consider upgrading via the 'pip install --upgrade pip' command.
```

3. I tried to import plyvel:
```
$ python3 -c 'import plyvel'
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/plyvel/__init__.py", line 6, in <module>
    from ._plyvel import (  # noqa
ImportError: /usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/plyvel/_plyvel.cpython-35m-x86_64-linux-gnu.so: undefined symbol: _ZN7leveldb9DestroyDBERKSsRKNS_7OptionsE
```

The packages mentioned in https://plyvel.readthedocs.io/en/latest/installation.html#build-and-install-plyvel are installed and the shared objects of libleveldb can be found using locate:
```
$ locate leveldb
/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libleveldb.a
/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libleveldb.so
/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libleveldb.so.1
/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libleveldb.so.1.18
```
And the plyvel extension library appears to link against it correctly:
```
$ readelf -d /usr/local/lib/python3.5/dist-packages/plyvel/_plyvel.cpython-35m-x86_64-linux-gnu.so

Dynamic section at offset 0x27cc0 contains 27 entries:
  Tag        Type                         Name/Value
 0x0000000000000001 (NEEDED)             Shared library: [libleveldb.so.1]
...
```

However, curiously I found in the libleveldb.so.1.18 the searched symbol only with a slightly *different* variation:

```
0000000000016c40 g    DF .text	0000000000000a94  Base        _ZN7leveldb9DestroyDBERKNSt7__cxx1112basic_stringIcSt11char_traitsIcESaIcEEERKNS_7OptionsE
```

This looks like the search thing, but with extra `__cxx1112basic_stringIcSt11char_traitsIcESaIcEEERKNS` inside. Any help will be appreciated.

issue_title: In optimally_align_text, we shouldn't consider a text's overlap with itself
issue_body:
In [this line](https://github.com/Phlya/adjustText/blob/4a43bf30ae785111900076ea20ffb156189e6f2f/adjustText/adjustText.py#L128), when calculating the overlaps of text `t` using alignment `a`, we're including the size of the overlap with `t` itself, as originally placed.

One undesirable effect of this is that, if there are several alignments that don't overlap any points or fall outside the axes, we'll prefer the one that is *most different* from the original alignment, because it will have the least self-intersection. e.g. if the user originally chose to place their texts with ha=left, va=bottom, we'll prefer to change it to ha=right, va=top.

issue_title: tabula java requirements v6 or v7.
issue_body:
Please add to the documentation of tabluapy that tabula requires java 6 or java 7  only!

I spent many hours trying to diagnose why I could not get tabula-py to run. I had java but it was not the correct version. 

From the tabula readme 

> ## Using Tabula
> First, make sure you have a recent copy of Java installed. You can
> download Java at https://www.java.com/download/ . Tabula requires
> a Java Runtime Environment compatible with Java 6 or Java 7.

To hack around other java versions installed I prepend the correct path to java 7 I installed
```
import sys
b = sys.path
sys.path = ['/opt/java/jre1.7.0_79/bin'] + b
```
And can now extract the tables. This would have saved a lot of time  if requirements said
` java 6 or java 7`

issue_title: Script examples bias path
issue_body:
Hi Yuhuang, i tried your library with a DAVIS240C using last version of libcAER (just wanted to try and see if it worked, i didn't want to change version) running the file in scripts/davis240_test.py, and it works besides a little problem with the bias file path. If i run the script from shell it doesn't find the bias parameters file, because you set the default search path for the json file as: 
> "./scripts/configs/davis240c_config.json"

but if you run it inside the script folder he can't find it. Changing to 
> "./configs/davis240c_config.json"

everything works without any change, so you can run the script directly. Eventually i would put in this function:

> def load_json(file_path):

something like this for the IOError:
```python
errorString = "Error when trying to load bias file, not found in the specified location"
print(errorString)
raise
```
So that we know there is a problem in the file location.

However, i don't know if you use spyder with Anaconda, but if you change the working directory directory to the  pyaer directory (the top level one), it gives the errors:
> ImportError: libcaer might not be in the LD_LIBRARY_PATH or your numpy might not be the required version.Try to load _libcaer_wrap.so from the package directory, this will provide more information.

It seems that when you do:
> from pyaer import libcaer

It has conflict between the local directory pyaer and the one you installed with pip, because he searches for libcaer in the local directory, maybe for some reasons it has higher priority. Just to tell you, maybe this can happen to others.

issue_title: pstree not displaying the full tree on windows
issue_body:
I've tried running pstree.py:

    import collections
    import sys

    import psutil


    def print_tree(parent, tree, indent=''):
        try:
            name = psutil.Process(parent).name()
        except psutil.Error:
            name = "?"
        print(parent, name)
        if parent not in tree:
            return
        children = tree[parent][:-1]
        for child in children:
            sys.stdout.write(indent + "|- ")
            print_tree(child, tree, indent + "| ")
        child = tree[parent][-1]
        sys.stdout.write(indent + "`_ ")
        print_tree(child, tree, indent + "  ")


    def main():
        # construct a dict where 'values' are all the processes
        # having 'key' as their parent
        tree = collections.defaultdict(list)
        for p in psutil.process_iter():
            try:
                tree[p.ppid()].append(p.pid)
            except (psutil.NoSuchProcess, psutil.ZombieProcess):
                pass
        # on systems supporting PID 0, PID 0's parent is usually 0
        if 0 in tree and 0 in tree[0]:
            tree[0].remove(0)
        print_tree(min(tree), tree)


    if __name__ == '__main__':
        main()

and all I've got on windows7 was this little subset:

    0 System Idle Process
    `_ 4 System
      `_ 600 smss.exe

What's the reason of it? `print(len(sorted(psutil.pids())))` is giving me 177... Ideally I'd like to get the same output than the one provided by [process explorer](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sysinternals/downloads/process-explorer) and displaying the full tree.

Also, it's kinda unrelated to this thread topic but I'd like to know whether it'd be possible to mimick the behaviour of [handle](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/sysinternals/downloads/handle#microsoft-handle-kb-articles) using psutil... I'm trying to solve this [problem](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51896247/how-to-unlock-files-folders-on-windows-using-python?noredirect=1#comment90745735_51896247) and I think psutil can be really helpful here.

Thanks in advance.

issue_title: Implementing TLS Extension for Certificate Transparency
issue_body:
Hi,

I am looking to implement the usage of the TLS Extension for Certificate Transparency. 
From openssl itself it can be queried like that:
`openssl s_client -serverinfo 18  -connect google.com:443 </dev/null 2>/dev/null | sed -n -e "/BEGIN SERVERINFO/,/END SERVERINFO/p"`

The SCT is only sent if the client signals the support for the extension per https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6962#section-3.3.1

Further methods to serve the SCT is via OCSP or directly inside the Certificate. The SCT included in the OCSP response can be parsed already form the response and I will send in a pull request after I cleaned it up a little bit. 

Where would I start to implement this feature?

Kind Regards.

issue_title: OAuth'ing a bot while it's running leads to no console output until restart
issue_body:
Here's a simple bot that I started writing:

```python
import asyncio

import discord


class IconScraperBot(discord.Client):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.token = 'token_here'

    def run(self):
        return super().run(self.token)

    async def on_ready(self):
        print('Logged in as')
        print(self.user.name)
        print(self.user.id)
        print('------')

    async def on_server_join(self, server):
        print('joining {0}'.format(server.name))

    async def on_server_remove(self, server):
        print('leaving {0}'.format(server.name))

    async def on_message(self, message, flag=None):
        print('Message received: {0}'.format(message.content))


bot = IconScraperBot()
bot.run()
```
The bot starts fine, I see the `print()` statements in `on_ready()`, and I can see the bot in the list of users in my server after I use an OAuth URL to approve it.

The issue is somewhere in the following sequence of events:

1. Start bot
2. Use OAuth URL to grant access to the bot to join my server

The bot joins the server, but there's absolutely no console output. Nothing when it joins the server, nothing when I send it a message, and nothing when I kick the bot.

**However**, if I _restart_ the bot after it's joined then I at least see console output from `on_message()` and `on_server_remove()`. For whatever reason, something bugs out in `on_server_join()` which probably freezes the whole bot. There's no visible crashing in the console, though - everything appears to be running fine.

I've confirmed this on both Python 3.4.2 and 3.5.1

issue_body: check_version sends ApiVersionRequest (Kafka 0.10) first that I think is totally ignored by Kafka 0.9, because max_in_flight_requests_per_connection is set to 1 check_version will not be able to send ListGroupsRequest but it will instead try to receive data from the socket blocking indefinitely (or until the connection is dropped on server side) on the socket recv() (because the server 0.9 won't reply to ApiVersionRequest).

issue_title: [WebUI] Not working when served from iframe
issue_body:
#### Description

I'm running glances in web server mode inside a docker container.
There's nginx in front of it with the reverse proxy rules as suggested in the wiki.

Glances' web page is then served in an iframe by [muximux](https://github.com/mescon/Muximux) (just my way of managingall the webservices from one place).

The pages loads blank and throws several errors in the console (logs attached).

##### Cause

Muximux uses a few files with the same name, notably "/js/main.js", so when it's time to load glances' one, the browser tries to be smart and uses the one from the cache, never hitting the rewrite rule and breaking the page.

This will impact any use within in iframe where the main page uses similar names. The chance of having names similar to "/js/main.js" is quite high.

#### Versions

* Glances (glances -V): 2.7.1
* PSutil (glances -V): 5.0.0
* Operating System (lsb_release -a): alpine 3.4 (inside a docker container)

#### Logs

The console errors http://pastebin.com/kBHxb6MF

issue_title: --obfuscate with class members
issue_body:
I was trying a simple test with a class, and I have noticed that although pyminifier does obfuscate class members within the class itself, it doesn't change calls from outside the class, here's an example to illustrate this:

file test.py
```
class Test:
    integer = 0
    letter = 'A'
    decimal = 1.9

    def __init__(self,integer, letter, decimal):
        self._integer = integer
        self._letter = letter
        self._decimal = decimal

    def getInteger(self):
        return self._integer

t = Test(1, "B", 2.62)

print(t.getInteger())
print(t.integer)
```

And here's the result using
```
pyminifier --ofuscate test.py
```

```
M=print
class o:
 m=0
 v='A'
 f=1.9
 def __init__(g,m,v,f):
  g._integer=m
  g._letter=v
  g._decimal=f
 def y(g):
  return g._integer
t=o(1,"B",2.62)
M(t.getInteger())
M(t.integer)
# Created by pyminifier (https://github.com/liftoff/pyminifier)
```

It can be noticed that although "integer" and "getInteger" do get obfuscated inside the class, on the call outside the class, they're not, and of course, this code can't run, because 't' object has no attribute 'getInteger', etc.

issue_title: Optional args/kwargs are handled incorrectly
issue_body:
All those methods with `network=None, masklen=None, packed=None` signature are improperly handled via C-ext.

While parsing args/kwargs with `PyArg_ParseTupleAndKeywords()` declaring arg/kwarg's signature with `s` leads to fill the value with `Py_None` object while the arg was explicitly passed as `None` from python. This could be eliminated declaring an arg/kwarg with `z` - that leads a `NULL` value in C code instead of `Py_None`

I've prepared a PR https://github.com/mjschultz/py-radix/pull/31

issue_title: pysave doesn't work for type character.
Deallocation Issue in Ftypes
issue_body:
If the same shared library is repeatedly used with allocatable outputs, it appears as if it is _sharing_ the memory between the results, so that subsequent results overwrite the first ones. It also means that the deallocation segfaults because the first pointer being released deallocates fine, but when the remaining ones try to deallocate it, it has already been deallocated.

I think it is still good to have every `FtypeResult` instance try to deallocate itself (it is cheap); we should just add a check for allocation before trying to deallocate. We probably also ought to investigate whether it is better to _copy_ the array results before the method is called again. If we do the copy, there is obvious overhead and the memory becomes managed by python. If we don't copy, the user's results will be confusing with values being overwritten. If they are aware of that, they could do the copy themselves. If they are not, we have to do it transparently...

- close the issue

issue_title: Support for format_time in tztime module
issue_body:
The tztime module from i3status introduced another ```format_time``` option to enable pango markup: https://i3wm.org/i3status/manpage.html#_tztime

As py3status handles the tztime module, this new option needs to be taken into account when parsing the time's format. Currently, when using ```format_time```, the time stops updating and adds the ```%Y,%m,%d``` formatstrings to the time (see [here](https://github.com/ultrabug/py3status/blob/master/py3status/__init__.py#L413)).

issue_title: Uncertainty predictions
issue_body:
Goal: Have the model tell us how uncertain it is for a given prediction

The median_absolute_error vs rmse method is gonna be tough. 

so instead, let's do easy. 
our final model will also implement a `.predict_uncertainty()` method. 

our finalmodelatc will have to have two models set in it. one of them will be our base predictor (make regression predictions). the other will make our uncertainty predictions. 

how we do this:
we will return two values: both the base prediction, and the uncertainty prediction. let's return a dictionary with two keys on it: `base_prediction`, and `uncertainty_prediction`. 
we will first use our base predictor to get a prediction (or predictions)
then we'll have to hstack those onto the sparse scipy matrices we're working with, while saving a value to be returned later
then we'll feed the combined data into our uncertainty predictor. 
finally, format the output, and return it. 

let's also create some kind of a scoring utils for this. 

API:
train_uncertainty_model=True
delta_to_qualify_as_uncertain=None
delta_unit=['percentage', 'absolute_value'] (defaulting to absolute value)

Parts look easy to integrate because you just need to pass training and testing data. Any opposition if I work on a PR referencing it?

issue_title: keras future: use nn for representational learning early in ensemble
issue_body:
neural nets are fantastic for representational learning (turning raw data inputs into features). gradient boosting is fantastic at turning good features into accurate predictions. 

why not use both?

have a nn that does something like [1, 0.5. 0.1] with an output layer. drop the output layer, and just get the features from the last hidden layer. 

for each row, feed these features into a gradient boosting model (along with all the other features we'd normally use). 

voila- both models are used for what they're best at!

issue_title: Remove exclusive_creation argument from write() function
issue_body:
This will revert the changes from #77.
Raising an error by default is useful in some cases, but it can also be harmful in other cases.

As far as I know, in most APIs the "write" function overwrites by default.
Also, e.g., the Unix commands `cp` and `mv` by default overwrite their target.

I think it's reasonable to assume that users expect overwriting behavior, therefore we should give it to them.

Once the default is changed to overwriting behavior, the argument `exclusive_creation` isn't that relevant anymore and it should be removed.

If users want to make sure that a file is not overwritten, they'll either have to check the existence first (e.g. with [`os.path.exists()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.path.html#os.path.exists)) or they can use `f = SoundFile(..., mode='x')`.

issue_title: Importing pytesseract module invoking wrong system calls
issue_body:
## System Info:
**pytesseract:** 0.24(Latest)
**Machine:** Raspberry pi with Stretch
**Tesseract :** 4.0.0-beta.1-318-gee2a
**Pillow:** 5.2.0

## Issue:
`import pytesseract` invoking wrong calls to multiple python modules.

## Root Cause:
`import Image` in **pytesseract.py** file of the **pytesseract module**. It is causing system compiler to search for Image.py which inturns making multiple calls to different python modules with name **Image.py**.

## Solution:
**Correct Module: PIL**
so, `from PIL import Image` is the right (and must be default) import instead of `import Image` , therefore,
```
try:
    import Image
except ImportError:
    from PIL import Image

```
### must change to:

```
try:
    from PIL import Image
except ImportError:
    import Image
```
in the source (**pytesseract.py**) file.

issue_title: Feature request: Less strict table parsing
issue_body:
Hey there! 
To get the following table:

Column1 | Column2
------: | :------
   Data | Data
   Data | Data

the Mistletoe parser would require the following code:
```
| Column1 | Column2 |
| ------: | :------ |
|    Data | Data    |
|    Data | Data    |
```

Many Markdown implementations I know allow the following:
```
| Column1 | Column2
| ------: | :------
|    Data | Data
|    Data | Data
```
and in fact, while typing this, I figured out that Github will even parse the following:
```
Column1 | Column2
------: | :------
   Data | Data
   Data | Data
```
I don't see a particular use for the latter, but the first one has often made it much easier for me to maintain visually appealing, ASCII-formatted tables where the last column contains content of very different lengths.

Would it be easy for mistletoe to support this as well? @miyuchina If this is not out of scope but you are busy, I can also try implementing this myself.

issue_title: Documentation bug: smart quotes
issue_body:
On the PyPI page for bumpversion, the documented defaults for settings which take strings surround the strings with smartquotes. These can't be copied directly to a `.bumpversion.cfg` file because you'll get this error if you do so:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/bin/bumpversion", line 11, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/bumpversion/__init__.py", line 695, in main
    config.write(log_config)
  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/ConfigParser.py", line 412, in write
    key = " = ".join((key, str(value).replace('\n', '\n\t')))
UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode character u'\u201c' in position 0: ordinal not in range(128)
```

I would like to request that all these smart quotes (and other other non-ascii characters in the docs) be replaced with ascii-compatible characters.

Unfortunately, after some more testing, it looks like this is actually PyPI's fault, rather than yours. Copying the default strings from the github display of the README gives real quotes. Maybe you could add some de-smartification code to the parser? Though that seems really silly.

TL;DR F*$% "smart" quotes.

issue_title: javascript code result and js2py code result not equal
[VM] Subtracting from object properties
issue_body:
Found a tricky one :)

This one works fine:

```py
eval('''var v=44; v-=12; throw v;''')  # throws 36
```

And this one does not:

```py
eval('''var o={p: 44}; o.p-=12; throw o.p;''')  # throws -36
```

After quite some head scratching I managed to find that the arguments are reversed in [sub_op function](https://github.com/PiotrDabkowski/Js2Py/blob/b1bbbeb0bcfef6f9548217f51caa4545545de550/js2py/internals/operations.py#L113) in the 2nd case. That has brought me to [STORE_MEMBER_OP](https://github.com/PiotrDabkowski/Js2Py/blob/b1bbbeb0bcfef6f9548217f51caa4545545de550/js2py/internals/opcodes.py#L421) where `value` and `get_member(left, name, ctx.space)` might need swapping (and perhaps once more three lines just above), but I'm totally guessing on this one.

If that's correct, I can provide a pull request with a fix if you like.

issue_title: Delete files after encryption.
issue_body:
Generally, when you encrypt files, it might make sense to delete the *original* and only keep the encrypted version (this is probably not the default behaviour the users expect, but a regularly used one).

In this scenario - if you encrypt many files, it may make sense to allow `crypto` to delete these input files after encryption has finished (e.g. `--delete` or `--remove`). Of course the user could also do this manually, but it seems like a sensible option to include into crypto. @chrissimpkins do you think this feature would make sense?

Obviously it would also be important to make sure that the encryption was really successful as discussed in issue #13

And extending that thought - it would really be great to be able to *securely* delete this file (on users request), but I haven't seen a pythonic way to do this (OS X for example has an *Finder* option to *Secure Empty Trash ...*, but I don't think you can call this from Python :laughing:). Any ideas?

issue_title: xl_col_to_name doesn't handle negative values well
issue_body:
XlsxWriter 1.0.5

Currently, `xlsxwriter.utility.xl_col_to_name` doesn't guard against negative (or overly large positive) input. In Python 3, you'll get some kind of return value which probably doesn't correspond to anything sensible:

```python
>>> from xlsxwriter.utility import xl_col_to_name
>>> xl_col_to_name(0)
'A'
>>> xl_col_to_name(-1)
''
>>> xl_col_to_name(-2)
'Y'
>>> xl_col_to_name(-30000)
'YHQE'
>>> xl_col_to_name(445254)
'YHQE'
>>>
```

In Python 2, negative input will actually put the function into an infinite loop.

I propose this function should either raise an exception or return `None` on negative input.

Personally, I would also guard against overly large positive values, but that's less clear cut because it might be nice to accommodate non-Excel software or future versions of Excel that could potentially have higher column limits.

issue_title: Notify correctly if parent confirmed status of mempool txs changes
issue_body:
If a mempool transaction touches an address, Electrum requires the address status to be a function of the confirmed / unconfirmed status of the transaction inputs.

In general this makes the address status a function of the confirmed state of transactions not touching the address itself, and hence it can potentially change as each new block is found. 

I don't believe electrum-server gets this subtlety correct either.  Fortunately it's not too hard to fix in ElectrumX.

issue_title: Use trollius (asyncio) instead of gevent.
issue_body:
Gevent is a pain to install, and many users do not get the benefits of ia-wrapper's concurrent functionality. Trollius is easy to install, so all of ia-wrapper's concurrent functionality can be provided by default -- no need to install gevent to use `Mine()`. Asyncio has also been included in the Python standard library (on a provisional basis) as of 3.4.

I have created a new branch to start working on this: https://github.com/jjjake/ia-wrapper/tree/mine

One of the biggest changes is how `Mine` is called and used. Rather than the `Mine` object being an iterable that yields `Item` objects, `Item` objects can be manipulated via callback's:

```python
>>> from internetarchive.mine import Mine
>>> identifiers = [x.strip() for x in open('itemlist.txt')]
>>> download = lambda item: item.download()
>>> miner = Mine(identifiers, callback=download)
>>> miner.run()
```

This  branch is currently a work in progress, and ideas and suggestions are welcome!

issue_title: TagsInputMixin and models with multiple manyToMany fields
issue_body:
The TagsInputMixin doesn't like cases where a model has more than one ManyToMany fields, but not all of them are for tagging. 

I could offer a pull request which adds a get_tag_fields() method that returns a list of fm2m fields that are to be used for tagging (or None for the default behavious of all tags). The default behaviour of this method is to look for a tag_fields attribute which should be an iterable containing the names of fields to be tagged. formfield_for_manytomany() then does a call to the parent method if tag_fields is defined and doesn't contain the current field - this allows one to specify in the ModelAdmin class which m2m fields should be tag fields.

issue_title: Make a faster DatabaseCache
issue_body:
Django's DatabaseCache is a bit slow:

1. On every set, it does cull check via `SELECT COUNT(*)`, which means a full tablescan
2. It uses longtext with base64-encoded data rather than blobs
3. It uses a couple queries per set/add, rather than the single one that MySQL can do with `INSERT... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE ...`

... so make a faster one!

issue_title: Confusing ChoiceItem behavior

Turns out that values and labels are created differently than I'd expect:
```
class Choices(DjangoChoices):
    foo = ChoiceItem(_("Footastic"))
    bar = ChoiceItem(_("Baraboom"))
```

In this example, `foo` and `bar` are labels and `Footastic` and `Baraboom` are values - I'd expect the opposite behavior.

I also noticed that explicitly passing the label is not enough
```
foo = ChoceItem(label=_("Footastic"))
```

This is confusing to me, because labels are usually more verbose as well as more prone to change, and theferore they are less suitable for use in code:
```
class PizzaCrust(DjangoChoices):
    extra_thick_chicago_style = ChoiceItem('thick')
```

Why labels, instead of values, are inferred from the attribute name?

issue_title: Create aliases for lists of actions
issue_body:
@byt3bl33d3r,

It would be cool to create some kind of **option aliases** that could **gather several 'profiled' actions**. 
For instance with the `enum4linux` tool, the `-a` switch does simple enumeration stuff (shares, users etc.):
```
Usage: ./enum4linux.pl [options] ip

Options are (like "enum"):
    -U        get userlist
    -M        get machine list*
    -S        get sharelist
    -P        get password policy information
    -G        get group and member list
    -d        be detailed, applies to -U and -S
    -u user   specify username to use (default "")  
    -p pass   specify password to use (default "")   

Additional options:
    -a        Do all simple enumeration (-U -S -G -P -r -o -n -i).
              This opion is enabled if you don't provide any other options
    -r        enumerate users via RID cycling
    -o        Get OS information
    -i        Get printer information
    -n        Do an nmblookup (similar to nbtstat)
```
As you imagine, it makes the user's life easier by typing less :)

Cheers.

```python
def validate_date(date: str) -> datetime:
    """

    valid_formats = [

    for format in valid_formats:  # format is built-in name, but no error
```

issue_title: Have shortcuts dynamically set Inherits same way as HostName

A matrix of machines with different users crossed with different keys. Machines most easily identified by the last few digits of their IP.

Desired config structure:
```
---
hosts:

    # ssh 6.60 hits here and fails
    "6.*":
      Hostname: "99.99.9%h" # correctly substitutes 6.60 for %h
      Inherits:  "99.99.9%h" # this %h doesn't get interpreted

      # ssh 6.38 does get to here and works
    6.38:
        Hostname: "99.99.9%h" # same substitution as shortcut
        Inherits:  "99.99.96.38" # hardwired Inherits does traverse the nesting 

    99.99.96.60:
        Inherits:
        - key1
        - user1 
    99.99.96.38:
        Inherits:
        - key0
        - user0

templates:
       user1:

         User: ubuntu

          IdentityFile: key1
```

issue_body: Events always provide the interface_id. We currently don't check for correctness of this, so we might actually receive events not intended for us.

issue_title: Add support for Home Assistant Entity Registry
issue_body:
Home Assistant has an Entity Registry where you can "statically" assign entity ids to device serial numbers or other unique ids.

Since the name resolving through pyhomematic takes quite long when starting Home Assistant it would be cool if we could register a device in the entity registry once it's name has been fetched, so resolving that device's name can be skipped in the future. The Philips Hue platform already contains an example on how to use the Entity Registry.

Benefits people would get from this:

* Faster startup times (entities will be available instantly at startup)
* Stable entity ids (IMHO this is a huge thing)
* Renaming entity ids for usage with Home Assistant without renaming them on CCU
* Less load on CCU during HASS restarts

I think since all HM devices have a serial number this would be a good unique id to use in the Entity Registry (maybe together with the channel number, if required).

For more information see the developer docs:

https://developers.home-assistant.io/docs/en/entity_registry_index.html

issue_title: Incorrect results for `de.com` domains
issue_body:
`tldextract` seems to be giving incorrect results for `de.com` domains. Its results are inconsistant with the [tld](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/tld) python library, and [tldjs](https://github.com/oncletom/tld.js) in Javascript.

Given a url `http://www.nachrichten.de.com`, we expect the `registered_domain` to be `nachrichten.de.com`, as `de.com` is in the Public Suffix List. However, using tldextract `2.2.0` I get the following:

```python
import tldextract
tldextract.extract('http://www.nachrichten.de.com').registered_domain
>> 'de.com'
```

The tld library currently resolves this domain correctly:
```python
import tld
tld.get_tld('http://www.nachrichten.de.com')
>> u'nachrichten.de.com'
```

Likewise tldjs gets the same result:
```javascript
tldjs.parse('http://www.nachrichten.de.com').domain
>> "nachrichten.de.com"
```

issue_title: Using str in AWS_HEADERS causes spaces to be escaped!
issue_body:
See: https://github.com/boto/boto/issues/2536 If I set AWS_HEADERS as {'Cache-Control': 'max-age=%d, s-maxage=%d, must-revalidate' % (AWS_EXPIRY, AWS_EXPIRY)} (notice spaces), it will render to 'max-age=604800,%20s-maxage=604800,%20must-revalidate'. Encoding the string as bytestring solved my problem on Django 1.7.7 and python3, but I didn't do any thorough testing. 
AWS_HEADERS = {
    'Cache-Control': str.encode(
        'max-age=%d, s-maxage=%d, must-revalidate' % (
        AWS_EXPIREY, AWS_EXPIREY))
}

issue_title: Provide a small cli script to titlecase inputs?
issue_body:
Hi,
It could be helpful to add a small script to this project, maybe called `titlecase`, which basically would put its entries to titlecase:
```
$ titlecase "this is the name of an article.pdf"
This Is the Name of an Article.pdf
```

## A small python script?
```python
from sys import argv
from titlecase import titlecase
if __name__ == '__main__':
    for v in argv[1:]:
        print(titlecase(v))
```
Of course, it could be improved with a documentation, command line arguments etc. (I would suggest using [docopt](https://github.com/docopt/docopt)).

## Or a bash alias?
For instance, I just added [this alias](https://bitbucket.org/lbesson/bin/src/master/.bash_aliases#cl-570) to my Bash profile, and it works fine:
```bash
alias titlecase="python -c \"from sys import argv; from titlecase import titlecase; print(titlecase('\n'.join(argv[1:])))\""
```

issue_title: Managing multiple connections with Binance Socket Manager
issue_body:
Hi,
Is there a way to handle multiple connections with a single Socket manager? 
Seems like after running ``bm.start()`` new sockets cannot be added, and ``bm.start()`` cannot be called twice in the same instance. 

Please have a look at the following example, I am creating two web sockets for **ADABTC** and **ETHBTC**. The sockets are closed after the first message and then I try to recreate the sockets, but it doesn't work as expected.

```

from binance.client import Client
from binance.enums import KLINE_INTERVAL_1MINUTE
from binance.websockets import BinanceSocketManager

client = Client('MY_KEY', 'MY_SECRET', {"timeout": 10})
bm = BinanceSocketManager(client)
started = False

def process_message(msg):
    # Close connections after first message
    global bm
    print(msg)
    bm.stop_socket('ethbtc@kline_1m')
    bm.stop_socket('adabtc@kline_1m')


while True:
    if not 'ethbtc@kline_1m' in bm._conns
        conn = bm.start_kline_socket('ETHBTC', process_message, interval=KLINE_INTERVAL_1MINUTE)
    if not 'adabtc@kline_1m' in bm._conns
        conn = bm.start_kline_socket('ADABTC', process_message, interval=KLINE_INTERVAL_1MINUTE)

    # Binance WebSocket Manager will start only once
    if not started:
        started = True
        bm.start()

    time.sleep(5)

```

issue_title: Inconsistent handing of strings between fields.Boolean and inputs.Boolean
issue_body:
[inputs.Boolean](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/master/flask_restplus/inputs.py#L483) does some logic on how to handle strings such as "0" and "1", converting them to False/True respectively.

[fields.Boolean](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/master/flask_restplus/fields.py#L446) does not do this same logic, and instead returns `bool(value)` using python's definitions of "Truth" which converts "0" and "1" both to True.

Proposed fix to have fields.Boolean instead `return inputs.boolean(value)` to be consistent in how flask-restplus handles string-> bool conversion.

issue_title: Please follow Semantic Versioning in the future
issue_body:
https://semver.org/

Your recent update to version `2.1.0` breaks backwards compatibility by swapping the order of parameters to the constructor and raising an exception in existing code where no such exception was raised on `2.0.x` versions of the library.

Per the semantic versioning guidelines, the "1" in `2.1.0` should imply that _new features_ were added which are _backwards compatible_ to previous `2.x.x` releases. This breaking change _should_ cause the version number to be upgraded to `3.0.0` as the API is not backwards compatible to a prior `2.x.x` release such as `2.0.18`

issue_title: Whitelist / Blacklist proposal
issue_body:
This is a design proposal for adding white and black lists to defender. The goal of adding these lists is so that we can always accept or block a set of usernames/ip's in a fast and flexible way.

### Two ways to store the White/Black lists

In order to keep this flexible, we will have two ways to store white/black list, a static way using settings variables, and a dynamic way using Redis. 

In order to make a change to one of the settings you will need to restart your application to take the changes, this isn't ideal, and should only be used for settings that you know won't change.

Adding values to Redis will allow us to change the settings while the application is running, with no reloads. It also allows you to get fancy with your blocking or accepting by programmatically adding or removing items from the Redis list when needed.

###  How it will work.

When a request comes in, it will check if the white/black list is enabled, if so, it will take the list from the Django settings and merge them with the list from Redis. The checks will be performed before we process the request, and if there are any white list matches, they will pass before we do any other checks. If there is a black list match then the request is blocked before they are processed.

### There will be 6 new settings added:

``DEFENDER_ENABLE_WHITELIST`` Boolean defaulted to False. If enabled it will look in the white list to see if the username or IP is in the white list before evaluating request. If it is in, it will bypass all checks.

``DEFENDER_ENABLE_BLACKLIST`` Boolean defaulted to False. If enabled it will look in the black list to see if the username or IP is in the black list before evaluating request. If it is in, it will automatically block the request.

``DEFENDER_IP_WHITELIST`` default empty list. The list of IP's that you want to skip the checks for. 

``DEFENDER_IP_BLACKLIST`` default empty list. The list of IP's that you want to always block.

- We should support CIDN: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Classless_Inter-Domain_Routing so that it is easy to add a whole subnet at once.
- We should try and support IPv6 from day one, if it doesn't make this more complicated.

``DEFENDER_USERNAME_WHITELIST`` default empty list. The list of username's that you want to skip the checks for. 

``DEFENDER_USERNAME_BLACKLIST`` default empty list. The list of username's that you want to always block.

### There will be 4 new Redis keys

There will be 4 new keys to store white and black lists, they will not have an expiration on them.

- ``prefix:whitelist:ip`` : list
- ``prefix:whitelist:username``  : list
- ``prefix:blacklist:ip``  : list
- ``prefix:blacklist:username``  : list

### Django admin changes

To make managing the white and black lists easier, we will add a new page to the Django admin where you can see the current white and black lists (settings and Redis) and the ability to edit (add and remove) the Redis lists.

This will give admins one place to see all of the settings, and make changes when needed.

### Questions?
- Which check should be first, white or black?
- Do we want to log the whitelist or black list request to the database?
- Should we add support for regular expressions to match username's, or just straight username strings?

issue_title: Unable to exclude many-to-many relationships
issue_body:
This seems to be a bit different from the other many-to-many issues here: I have a many-to-many relationship on a versioned table that I don't want tracked by continuum.

Here's a paired down version of the models:

```
from sqlalchemy import Table, Column, DateTime, String, Integer, ForeignKey, Text, func
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship, backref

from meta import Base 

channel_tag = Table('service_channel_service_tag', Base.metadata,
    Column('channel_id', Integer, ForeignKey('service_channel.id'), primary_key=True),
    Column('tag_id', Integer, ForeignKey('service_tag.id'))
)

class ServiceChannel(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'service_channel'
    __versioned__ = {
        'exclude': ['tags']
    }

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    title = Column(Text(convert_unicode=True))
    description = Column(Text(convert_unicode=True))

    tags = relationship("ServiceTag",
                        secondary=channel_tag,
                        backref="channels")

    def __repr__(self):
        return "<ServiceChannel(id='%s')>" % (self.id)

class ServiceTag(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'service_tag'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    tag = Column(String(30), unique=True, nullable=False)

    def __repr__(self):
        return "<ServiceTag(id='%s', tag='%s')>" % (self.id, self.tag)
```

In this particular case, having the historical data of tags is unnecessary. Is there some obvious way to have continuum ignore this that I'm missing?

issue_title: NoReverseMatch exception thrown for custom primary keys
issue_body:
If you attempt to attach DjangoObjectActions to a model that has a primary key that is neither hex nor decimal, a `NoReverseMatch` exception is thrown.

The problem is this code in `_get_action_urls()`:

```
            # change, supports pks that are numbers or uuids
            url(r'^(?P<pk>[0-9a-f\-]+)/actions/(?P<tool>\w+)/$',
```

This seems arbitrary, considering that we're creating a new custom URL space and all primary keys have to be already URL-compatible.

I fixed my use case by relaxing the pattern:
```
            url(r'^(?P<pk>[\w\\-]+)/actions/(?P<tool>\w+)/$',
```
...which doesn't appear to have any negative consequences.

Recommendation:

1. Relax the regex; or

2. Provide a mechanism to easily override the default assumptions about primary key regex; or

3. Make it easier to add extra urls to the results of this function.  I tried to create a subclass that appended another URL pattern to the results of `super(DjangoObjectActions, self)._get_action_urls()` but I needed a bunch of context that's created within that method, so it was just easier to copy the entire method into the subclass and change the regex.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/jerojasro/.venvs/tpaga-api/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django/core/handlers/exception.py", line 39, in inner
    response = get_response(request)
  File "/home/jerojasro/.venvs/tpaga-api/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django/core/handlers/base.py", line 216, in _get_response
    response = self.process_exception_by_middleware(e, request)
  File "/home/jerojasro/.venvs/tpaga-api/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django/core/handlers/base.py", line 214, in _get_response
    response = response.render()
  File "/home/jerojasro/.venvs/tpaga-api/lib/python3.5/site-packages/django/template/response.py", line 109, in render
    self.content = self.rendered_content
  File "/home/jerojasro/.venvs/tpaga-api/lib/python3.5/site-packages/rest_framework/response.py", line 72, in rendered_content
    ret = renderer.render(self.data, accepted_media_type, context)
  File "/home/jerojasro/.venvs/tpaga-api/lib/python3.5/site-packages/rest_framework_swagger/renderers.py", line 21, in render
    data = self.get_openapi_specification(data)
  File "/home/jerojasro/.venvs/tpaga-api/lib/python3.5/site-packages/rest_framework_swagger/renderers.py", line 34, in get_openapi_specification
    type(data)
AssertionError: Expected a coreapi.Document, but received <class 'NoneType'> instead.
```

The `get_openapi_specification` function should (maybe) be prepared to handle a `None` as its `data` argument.

Or maybe django-rest-framework should be fixed to return an empty `Document` in this situation, instead of `None`, but I'm not sure which one would be the semantically correct solution.

For what is worth, changing django-rest-framework to give back an empty `Document` does not break its tests (see https://github.com/jerojasro/django-rest-framework/commit/0f6edde2acf97f1ecc4411994563a28e4b162598 ), and fixes this issue.
